column2: My Creativity Map - They reflect on the times they have been creative in different domains (school, home, nature)
column3: To not find the idea of being creative intimidating by reflecting on the times they have been creative
column6: What Could this be? - Students see different shapes and quickly imagine what each shape could become under a time limit
column7: Removes fear of “wrong”. Shows ideas aren’t linear.
column10: Riddle Remix - Explore different solutions to riddles
column11: Develop flexible thinking by exploring multiple possible answers
column14: Grid Logic Challenge - Students tackle puzzles with all information provided
column18: Category Detective - Identify shared categories between items
column26: Impossible Problem Challenge - Give kids a seemingly impossible problem and ask them to brainstorm for ideas
column27: Interpret and create graphs to represent relationships between variables

column15: Learn that systems (including AI and computers) work using conditions
column22: AI Basics - As a tool, misinformation, reliability, principles
column23: Understand that AI is a tool, not a source of guaranteed truth
column26: What's the Catch? - Spot misleading offers, hidden catches, and data-safety risks
column27: Build early data and digital safety awareness

column2: Active listening - discussion on what makes a good listener, which traits to keep and discard, analyse situations to determine if they are active listeners or not, takeaway exercise to be a good listener and share experience in the next class
column3: Develop the ability to listen with focus, understand others better, and respond thoughtfully.
column6: Prompt Engineering - Cover materials to discuss what prompts are, quiz to make sure they understand, and experiment how changes in prompts influence AI-generated responses
column7: Develop the skill of giving clear, specific, and structured directions
column10:
You Can Only Pick Two Game - Choose 2:
1. Cheap
2. Fast
3. High Quality
column14: (Communication) Precision Instructions - Giving instructions precisely to systems
column15: Learn how systems (including AI) rely on precise input
column22: Prompting like a Pro - CLEAR Framework + One shot/Few shot prompt
column23: Improve vague prompts into clear, structured ones
column27: Preparation, organization and time-managent before an event

column6: Parrot, Paraphrase, Power-Up - One student speaks and the other practices active listening by repeating the message exactly (parrot), rephrasing it in their own words (paraphrase), and then suggesting an improved version (power up).
column14: Strategic Puzzles - Students solve puzzles by comparing rules and predicting which gives the best chance of winning
column15: Compare different rule systems and their outcomes
column19: Apply simple probability concepts to solve problems and make predictions using data
column22: AI Dungeon Game - Students quickly see how different prompt inputs lead to very different narrative results
column23: Develop cause-and-effect awareness between instructions and AI responses
column26: Scenario Agents - Students revisit the agent planner activity and learn to reduce mistakes by designing clearer instructions
column27: Students revisit the agent planner activity and learn to reduce mistakes by designing clearer instructions

column3: First AI insight: AI is fast, can make mistakes, better intructions lead to better outcomes
column6: Multiple Uses Game - Students are given everyday objects and challenged to come up with as many different uses as possible within a time limit.
column7: Reduce fear of being “wrong” in idea generation
column11: Understand that effective solutions are based on real user needs, and learn basic methods to gather insights about people’s problems and behaviors
column15: Practice solving problems using structured logic
column26: “Future You” Vision Board - Kids create a vision board for what their project could look like in the real world
column27: Apply step-by-step logical thinking to improve clarity and reduce errors in project workflows

column6: Product Building - Write down your idea for the product you want to build
column15: Experiment with how changing inputs changes results
column18: AI, Classify This! - Prompt an AI system to group diverse items based on logical criteria and analyze the reasoning behind each classification
column19: Practice giving AI clear logical classification rules
column22: Hallucination Hunt - Students ask AI 3 questions: • obvious • tricky • emotional They tag AI answers: 🟢 safe / 🟡 unsure / 🔴 dangerous
column23: Identify when AI responses may be unreliable
column26: Demo Day Checklist - Test the technology, materials, and timing, preparing for a smooth and professional booth setup
column27: Apply organizational and planning skills to ensure a professional and efficient presentation

column2: Estimation Challenge Quiz - Simple, fast-paced math problems
column3: Build number sense and confidence in thinking quickly and logically
column6: Idea Round - Students ask AI: “Give 10 uses for a paper clip.” They compare: • Which ideas feel boring? • Which feel original? • Which feel impossible?
column7: First AI insight: AI is fast, can make mistakes, better intructions lead to better outcomes
column10: User Interview Lab - Students create 5 interview questions for a potential user of their product idea
column11: Develop skills in asking open-ended, insight-focused questions to uncover real user challenges and behaviors
column14: Which Way is Best? - Students are given different scenarios to understand the optimal decision to make and explore alternative strategies with AI and assess outcomes
column15: Evaluate multiple possible solutions before making a decision

column10: Pain Point Ranking Challenge - Students list problems they discovered during user interviews, then score each one based on how often it happens (frequency) and how serious it is (impact). They calculate a priority score and decide which single problem is most important to solve first
column11: Analyze user research findings, evaluate problems based on frequency and impact, and prioritize which user need to address first using structured decision-making
column13: 🧠 Artifact  (think on this at home and discuss)
column14: Ad-Maker Challenge - AI generates slogans → students filter manipulative ones.
column15: Develop awareness of ethical communication boundaries

column19: Students create a mini pitch (4–5 slides/pages) about their product

column2: Emotional Questions to AI - Write and ask emotional questions to AI

column10: AI as a Simulated User - Use AI as a user and raise questions and solve problems
column11: Use AI to simulate user perspectives, identify hidden weaknesses in ideas, and refine solutions based on realistic user needs

column2: Reverse-It - Students spin two wheels to get an object and a twist, then re-imagine how that object could look and work in a new way.
column3: Develop flexible thinking by breaking the “this is how it’s supposed to be” mindset
column6: Reclassify Everything - Students group everyday objects based on unexpected or unusual traits
column7: Understand that categories depend on the rules we choose
column10: Creative Pattern Inventions - students study real-world patterns and break them down into what repeats and what changes.
column11: Develop skills in observing structure, not just details
column14: Redesign Conversations - Turn ordinary small talk into interesting, curious questions
column18: Clarity Speed Round - Describe a simple object in 3 different ways (to a toddler, a scientist, and an alien)
column19: Learn to adjust explanations based on audience knowledge level
column22:
Say It Without Saying It - 
Students must communicate a message without using key words.
Ex: Explain “climate change” without saying “earth, hot, temperature, pollution.
column23: Develop precise communication skills under constraints

column2: Claim Builder - Learn how to make premises and conlusions logically using claims
column3: Learn how to build logical arguments and understand how strong reasoning works
column11: Understand that sets are groups based on shared properties
column14: Social Deduction Mysteries - Engage in activities that require observation, inference, and collaboration to get to the answer
column18: Build the Bridge - Can you explain your project so that anyone — even a grandparent — can understand it?
column19: Practice explaining ideas in simple, relatable language

column10:
Venn Thinking - How Categories Overlap - 
Before Set Theory formally — introduce overlapping traits
column11: Learn to visualize similarities and differences clearly
column14: Navigating Conflict Activity - Scenarios to determine what the optimal way of resolution is
column15: Learn strategies for resolving disagreements constructively
column19: Learn that effective persuasion combines logic, clarity, and connection
column23: Understand why humans don’t act rationally

column2: Fact, Trick, or Opinion Quiz - Distinguish objective facts from opinions and misleading claims
column3: Learn to tell the difference between facts, opinions, and misleading information
column7: Practice turning ideas into logical decision flows
column10:
Odd One Out — But Defend It - 
Show 4 items (e.g., cat, lion, dolphin, shark).
Students choose the odd one out but must justify their logic.
column11: Practice justifying answers using clear reasoning
column14: Team Tower - students work in teams to clearly explain what they’ve learned and built at Ground Zero in an engaging way to notice how their message lands, and how the audience reacts, and reflect
column18: One Idea, Many Voices - Express the same idea for different audiences (parent, teacher, stranger) and tones (🙂 friendly | 🎓 formal | 🚀 persuasive)
column19: Understand how tone changes meaning and impact
column22: Agent Mission Briefing - students examine a well-crafted prompt to understand how it guides an AI agent
column23: Understand how structured prompts guide AI agent behavior

column19: Experiment with persuasive techniques like strong openings, benefits, and emotional appeal

column2: If/Then Chains - Build a chain of logical consequences using If/Then reasoning.
column3: Understand cause-and-effect relationships in ideas and decisions
column6: Chain of Reasoning - Students map thinking steps that link a claim to a clear conclusion.
column7: Practice explaining how we know something, not just what we think
column10: Same Thing, Different Groups - Give students one object. They must place it into 3 different categories, and explain why each is correct.
column11: Realize that multiple answers can be logically valid
column14: Point of View Carousel: Argue the other Side - Through guided debate exercises and clear signposting, students practice articulating ideas and understanding multiple perspectives
column15: Develop empathy by articulating opposing views
column18:
AI Tone Transformer - Students give AI a sentence and ask it to rewrite:
- persuasive
- funny
- emotional
- formal
column19: Recognize that communication is shaped by style, emotion, and intent, not just content

column18: AI Hook - Ask AI to generate responses for each audience and ask it to start with a strong hook. Reflect on hooks and their effectiveness.
column19: Analyze how different hooks capture attention
column22: Agent Planner - Design step-by-step plans for AI agents, incorporating tools, verification checks, and fallback options to ensure reliable outcomes.
column23: Learn to design AI workflows that include verification checks

column2: Pattern Puzzles - Solve visual and numerical puzzles to practice spotting patterns and relationships
column3: Strengthen problem-solving skills by spotting patterns and connections
column7: Understand that AI learns by being given examples and labels

column14: Upgrade your AI Pitch - students pitch their AI project, get feedback, and revise their pitch to make it stronger

The High-Stakes AI Decision Room - Students act as a decision team using AI in time-sensitive scenarios where:

choices affect outcomes
bias can distort judgment
cooperation matters
prompts must be precise
